title: Locally Differentially Private Graph Clustering via the Power Iteration Method

reviews.summary: The paper proposes an interactive edge-local differential privacy (LDP) algorithm for two-way graph clustering based on a privatized power-iteration scheme. The key ideas are to (i) replace the random walk matrix by its “lazy” variant, (ii) eliminate the dominant uniform eigencomponent by subtracting the all-ones term, and (iii) calibrate per-iteration Laplace noise using a lower bound on the minimum degree estimated privately. Under assumptions on minimum degree, eigengap, and entrywise delocalization of the second eigenvector, the authors argue that a constant privacy budget suffices for accurate recovery on well-clustered graphs; experiments on SBM and a large Reddit core support improvements over randomized-response (RR) + spectral clustering.
strengths: Adapting power iteration clustering (PIC) to the edge-LDP setting with multi-round interaction is timely and interesting, given the known limitations of non-interactive RR for general graphs.
Eliminating the leading eigencomponent via subtracting 1/n J is a simple but impactful device in the LDP iterative setting, reducing the amplification of noise tied to the dominant eigenvector.
The algorithm is designed to be memory-light (O(n) rather than O(n^2) at the server), which is a genuine practical advantage over storing a privatized dense adjacency.
The paper provides comparisons against the standard RR + spectral baseline on SBMs across privacy budgets and graph sizes.
It includes a large real-world graph (Reddit) experiment to probe scalability and robustness, and ablation-style discussion about the role of iteration count and spectral gap.

The high-level motivation for moving from non-interactive RR to interactive protocols in edge-LDP is well articulated, and the role of each algorithmic “difference” relative to non-private PIC is explained.

Addressing clustering under edge-LDP is important, and interactive protocols are underexplored relative to one-shot RR releases; if the guarantees can be solidified, this would be a useful addition to the literature.
weaknesses: The minimum-degree condition assumed (at least 2√n log^4 n) appears unrealistically large for the n used in experiments, and in many practical regimes; it is unclear whether the algorithm (especially Step 3’s degree-raising) is even executable for moderate n without adding more than n edges per node.

The privacy proof (Theorem 4.1) does not carefully account for the sensitivity dependence on degree changes (d_i varies when an edge flips) and on the deterministic degree-raising pre-processing (Step 3), which can change the function in ways not bounded by the stated sensitivity.

Several definitions and constants are internally inconsistent (e.g., the per-iteration ε allocation and Laplace scales; the parameter g used to define T and eigengap), casting doubt on the formal guarantees.

The sole baseline is RR + spectral, despite a growing set of private spectral mechanisms (even if often in central-DP). A stronger empirical section would include, at minimum, other spectral pipelines adapted to edge-LDP (e.g., debiasing downshifts) and stress tests on sparser SBMs.
The evaluation metric compares to the (non-private) spectral clustering output, not to ground truth on synthetic data; misclassification/overlap against planted labels should be reported for SBMs.

Figures contain inconsistencies (axes with negative “p” or “q”, possibly OCR issues), and some claims (e.g., randomized response performing “randomly” in certain ranges) are not substantiated with variance/error bars.

There are pervasive notation slips and contradictions: λ-indexing, use of B vs. W, the exact definition and sign of g, and the iteration count T. These hinder reproducibility and confidence in the analysis.
The role of clipping (c) in both privacy and utility is underexplained; choices in experiments (c=5 or 10) deviate from what is claimed necessary in theory.

The paper does not sufficiently contrast with recent private power iteration methods under edge-DP in centralized models (e.g., noisy power methods) or with local DP spectral pipelines that include debiasing (even if theoretically different), to clearly position what is new vs. what is adapted.

The negative results in the literature (e.g., ε = Ω(log n) under RR on general graphs) are cited, but it remains unclear where, precisely, the proposed interactive-LDP method breaks the barrier and which assumptions are essential vs. merely convenient.
reviews.questions:
1. Please provide a rigorous sensitivity bound for the Line 6 query that accounts for (a) the change in d_i when a_{i,j} flips and (b) the deterministic degree-raising Step 3. How do you ensure ε-edge-LDP with respect to the original adjacency lists when Step 3 may add a data-dependent number of edges?
2. Theorem 4.1 states “the privacy budget for each publication at Line 6 is 9/10 · δ/T,” which appears dimensionally inconsistent. What is the exact ε allocation per round and the corresponding Laplace scale, and how do these relate to the sensitivity bound you prove?
3. The iteration count is set as T = 2 log n / log g while later g is instantiated as 0.005 on Reddit, making log g < 0. Could you clarify the definition and intended range of g, and restate the eigengap condition in a standard way (e.g., λ_3 ≤ λ_2/g with g > 1)?
4. The minimum-degree requirement (≥ 2√n log^4 n) seems infeasible for n ≈ 10^4 used in experiments. Did Step 3 actually add edges in your runs? If not, what δ was used in practice, and how does that reconcile with the theory?
5. For SBMs, could you report misclassification/overlap against ground truth (up to a global flip) and include stronger RR baselines (e.g., downshift/debias before spectral) to isolate the gains due to interaction?
6. You subtract (1/n)∑_j x_j^{(t-1)} each round to eliminate v_1. In practice, this mean is computed from already noisy x vectors. Does this affect the spectral target (i.e., are you implementing W̃ exactly) and how is this error controlled in your analysis?
7. Broadcasting the full x^(t) to all users each round incurs O(n log n) communication per user. Can the protocol be modified to send only neighbor entries (or a sketch) without breaking privacy/utility? Any empirical measurements of communication cost?
reviews.assessment: The paper tackles an important problem—community detection under edge-LDP—by introducing an interactive, power-iteration-based algorithm that is memory efficient and, in spirit, well motivated. The elimination of the leading eigenvector in an LDP iterative computation is a nice idea, and the experimental signs of improvement over RR are promising. However, the submission in its current form has significant issues: (i) the privacy analysis appears incomplete, especially around the deterministic degree-raising step and sensitivity to degree changes; (ii) the theoretical presentation contains several inconsistencies (ε allocation, eigengap parameter g, indexing of eigenvectors) that undermine confidence; and (iii) the experimental evaluation, while encouraging, lacks key baselines and reports a metric that does not use ground truth on SBMs. The strong (and arguably impractical) minimum-degree assumption further narrows applicability, and it is unclear whether the experiments satisfy the theoretical conditions. With these concerns, I cannot recommend acceptance at ICML at this time. I encourage the authors to tighten the privacy proof (or redesign pre-processing to avoid pitfalls), clarify all constants/assumptions, add ground-truth-based evaluations and stronger baselines, and discuss communication-efficient variants. If addressed, the work could make a meaningful contribution to interactive LDP methods for graphs.
